source: Numerics/Data/Interpolation/Support/Search.cs; the ascending cases below predate any upstream test file (grep-confirmed across Test_Numerics at curation time). The `*_descending` cases are adapted from the new v2.1.4 Test_Search.cs (DescendingSearchesAgreeWithSequential/NonzeroStartsReturnTheSequentialBracket), which added the first upstream regression coverage for Search's descending-order behavior.
reference: Numerics @ 2a0357a. Args convention (this port's own -- see fixtures/README.md): args = [values..., x, start] (n = len(args) - 2). The plain `sequential`/`bisection` targets use SortOrder.Ascending (the only order exercised by any real caller -- SNIS.cs always calls Search.Sequential with the default order, and Histogram.GetBinIndexOf always calls Search.Bisection with the default order too); the `*_descending` targets exercise SortOrder.Descending explicitly (see search.hpp's file header: v2.1.4 fixed bisection()'s descending branch, previously dead code that always returned `start` regardless of where `x` actually fell). Probes cover both boundary sentinels (before/after the range, exact first/last-element match), interior lookups, and one non-zero `start` case per direction. Every value was generated by running the case's inputs through tools/oracle_emitter --dump against the REAL C# Search class and confirming the printed actuals reproduce via verify_oracles.py.

curated via oracle_emitter --dump; genuinely start-sensitive: x=7's true bracket is index 1 (between values[1]=5 and values[2]=10, the answer a start=0 scan finds), but scanning forward from start=3 skips it -- the loop's first hit is i=3 (values[3]=20 >= 7), returning i-1=2

curated via oracle_emitter --dump; genuinely start-sensitive: with start=0, bisection would correctly bracket x=7 at index 1, but xlo is seeded at start=3 and the loop only ever narrows xhi toward it (never revisits indices below start), so it returns 3
